macro_segments.title: The Principle of Repetition at Har Sinai: Laws Said to Bnei Noach and Repeated or Not Repeated

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Ever Min Hachai and the Question of Why Repeat Prohibitions Given to Bnei Noach
The Core Principle: Anything Said to Bnei Noach and Repeated at Har Sinai Applies to Both; Only to Bnei Noach and Not Repeated Applies Only to Israel
The Gemara's Challenge: If Not Repeated at Har Sinai, Should It Apply Only to Bnei Noach?
Resolution: The Principle That Nothing Can Be Forbidden to Bnei Noach and Permitted to Israel

macro_segments.title: Exceptions to the Principle: Yefat Toar, Less Than a Pruta, and the Role of Mechila

micro_segments.title: The Case of Yefat Toar: Permitted for Israel but Forbidden for Bnei Noach—Resolution via Kibush (Conquest)
Less Than a Pruta: Forbidden for Bnei Noach but Permitted for Israel—Resolution via Mechila (Forgiveness)
Tosafot's Analysis: These Are Not True Exceptions but Differences in Weight and Circumstance

macro_segments.title: The Issue of Abortion: Is It Forbidden for Both or Only Bnei Noach? Tosafot and Rav Moshe Feinstein

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's Question: Why Doesn't the Gemara Raise Abortion as a Counter-Example?
Tosafot's Answer: Abortion Is Not Fully Permitted for Israel; The Distinction Is Not Between Permitted and Forbidden but Between Ranks of Prohibition
The Case of Saving the Mother's Life: When Abortion Is a Mitzvah for Israel but Forbidden for Bnei Noach
Tosafot's Alternative: Even for Bnei Noach, Saving the Mother's Life May Be Permitted Despite Considering the Fetus Alive

macro_segments.title: Milah (Circumcision): Given to Avraham and Not to All Bnei Noach; Questions About When Repetition at Sinai Counts

micro_segments.title: The Counter-Example of Milah: Repeated at Sinai Yet Applies Only to Israel, Not to Bnei Noach
Resolution: Repetition Only for Detail, Not the Core Mitzvah, Does Not Count as True Repetition
The Case of Peru V'Rivu (Procreation): Also Given to Bnei Noach, Repeated at Sinai, Yet Applies Only to Israel
The Gemara's Resolution: Milah Was Given Only to Avraham and His Line, Not to All Bnei Noach; Peru V'Rivu Needed Explicit Retraction at Sinai
Rav Oshaya's Challenge: If Milah Was Only for Avraham's Line, Why Did Avraham Circumcise Keturah's Children?
Rabbi Yosi's Answer: Avraham Circumcised All His Children at That Time, but the Eternal Mitzvah Applies Only to Yitzchak's Line

macro_segments.title: Adam HaRishon and the Prohibition of Eating Meat: When Meat Was Permitted and When It Was Forbidden

micro_segments.title: The Statement of Rav Yehuda: Adam HaRishon Was Not Permitted to Eat Meat, Only Vegetation
When Bnei Noach Arrived: Meat Became Permitted with the Prohibition of Ever Min Hachai (Limb of a Living Animal)
Derivation from the Pasuk: The Details of Ever Min Hachai and Exceptions Like Sheratzim (Rodents)

macro_segments.title: The Utility of Animals and the Lost Potential of the Serpent: Mastery Over Creation Before and After Sin

micro_segments.title: Counter-Examples: Can You Use Fish and Birds to Do Work?
Rabbi Shimon ben Menasia's Lament: What a Great Servant the Serpent Was Before It Sinned
The Gemara's Digression: Meat from Heaven for Rabbi Shimon ben Chalafta and the Question of Kashruth

macro_segments.title: Blasphemy (Birkat Hashem): The Court Case Process and Who Must Tear Their Garments

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Case: Witnesses Testify Using Euphemisms Until Final Testimony with God's Explicit Name
The Requirement for God's Four-Letter Name: Rav Achai bar Yaakov's Position
Why Stand When Hearing God's Name? The Derasha from the Pasuk About King Eglon

macro_segments.title: Keriah (Tearing Garments): When It Is Required and When the Tear Remains Unsewn

micro_segments.title: The Source of Keriah: The Story of Ravshakeh Blaspheming God Before King Chizkiyahu
The Gezera Shava: Connection Between Keriah for Blasphemy and Keriah for a Parent—Both Unsewn

macro_segments.title: Who Must Perform Keriah and Conditions: Hearing Firsthand vs. Secondhand; Jews vs. Non-Jews; Explicit Name vs. Kinui

micro_segments.title: Hearing Firsthand vs. Secondhand: Both Require Keriah, But Witnesses Who Heard Originally Do Not Repeat It
Rav Yehuda in the Name of Shmuel: Keriah Only for a Jew Using God's Explicit Name
Rabbi Chiya's Alternative: In Principle Keriah Even for a Non-Jew with Any Name of God; Nowadays We Exempt Because It's Too Common

macro_segments.title: The Second and Third Witness: When They Say 'I Also Agree' and the Question of Valid Testimony

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Derivation: Technically 'Me Too' Works Even Though Rabbis Usually Require More; Here It's Necessary to Avoid Repetition of the Curse
The Second Witness's 'Af Ani Kamohu' (I Also Agree): Whether Saying 'Me Too' Suffices as Valid Testimony
The Third Witness: Required to Ensure Valid Testimony Structure and Consequences Under Rabbi Akiva's View

macro_segments.title: Avoda Zara (Worship of Foreign Gods): Forms of Worship Punishable by Death vs. Other Transgressions

micro_segments.title: Classic Forms of Avoda Zara: Slaughtering, Burning Incense, Pouring Libations, Prostration
Accepting a Foreign God as One's God: Even Without Physical Acts, Verbal Acceptance Constitutes Avoda Zara
Acts of Adoration That Are Negative Prohibitions But Not Punishable by Death: Hugging, Kissing, Sweeping, Anointing, Clothing
Vowing and Swearing by a Foreign God's Name: Negative Prohibition But Not Avoda Zara
Unique Forms of Worship: Baal Peor and Merkolis—Whatever Is the Actual Form of Worship for That Idol
